narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 66.6% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt—a risk level that led to the rejection to protect the program's outcomes. As a Black male with a low family income bracket, full-time work plans, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, and an LSAT score of 26.50, your profile was evaluated against thousands of similar students, and unfortunately, it aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar. The model's decision hinged primarily on your performance in later undergraduate years, where your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile of your school—far below the typical mid-range standing—pushing strongly toward a failure prediction by signaling potential challenges in sustaining high-level legal coursework. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, which, though slightly below the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by suggesting a baseline academic capability that nudged the prediction somewhat toward passing. Your Year 1 grades, also in the bottom decile compared to peers' average mid-decile performance, added further weight toward the failure side, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum. These three factors—your Year 3 decile ranking as the top driver, followed by GPA positively and Year 1 decile negatively—overrode other elements like your LSAT and family background in shaping this outcome. While full-time status is common for most applicants, your lower deciles stood out as unusually weak compared to the dataset's averages around the 5th or 6th decile. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience, especially given the systemic hurdles many Black men from low-income backgrounds face in competitive fields like law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the typical 37 average, strengthening your grades through targeted undergrad coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring part-time options or scholarships that align better with bar success patterns. Building a stronger academic track record now could flip the model's assessment in future applications, opening doors at schools that value your unique perspective. You're capable of turning this around—many have.
